name: Adad Extended
description: Adad, also known as Ishkur, was a prominent Mesopotamian storm god, associated with thunder, lightning, rain, and winds. He was a vital deity, as he controlled the life-giving rains that sustained agriculture and determined the prosperity of the land. However, his power was double-edged; he could also unleash devastating storms, floods, and droughts. As such, he was both revered and feared. He was considered the son of Anu, the sky god, and Antu, but other traditions identify him as the son of Enlil. He was often depicted riding a bull or chariot, wielding thunderbolts, and accompanied by winds and rain. Adad's consort was Shala, a goddess of grain and compassion, highlighting the duality of his nature. He was worshipped throughout Mesopotamia, from Sumer and Akkad to Assyria and Babylonia, each region adding their unique nuances to his character and attributes. Temples and shrines were dedicated to him in various cities, where priests performed rituals to appease him and ensure favorable weather conditions. Adad's role extended beyond the purely meteorological; he was also considered a god of justice and divination, lending his authority to oaths and contracts. His influence was felt in all aspects of Mesopotamian life, from the success of harvests to the outcome of battles. He embodied the unpredictable forces of nature and the delicate balance between prosperity and destruction.
subtitle: Mesopotamian Storm God of dual nature

keyMyths.title: Adad and the Great Flood
keyMyths.description: While not always directly responsible, Adad's storms often contributed to the devastating floods described in Mesopotamian myths, requiring appeasement to avert disaster.

keyMyths.title: Adad's Justice
keyMyths.description: Adad was invoked in oaths and treaties, believed to punish those who broke their word with storms and misfortune, ensuring justice.

keyMyths.title: Adad and the Harvest
keyMyths.description: Farmers prayed to Adad for timely rains to ensure bountiful harvests, highlighting his vital role in the agricultural cycle.
